original_hypothesis.title: DoS of NFT withdrawals via oversized erc20s array input (gas exhaustion) blocks legitimate withdrawal operations
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LiquidInfrastructureNFT.withdrawBalances/withdrawBalancesTo accept arbitrary-length `erc20s` arrays. The function loops over the entire array and performs balanceOf/transfer calls per entry. An approved operator could supply a very large list to force out-of-gas, preventing timely withdrawals in contexts where a relayer or other contract expects success.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an input-amplification DoS surface. While only owner/approved can call, it can still be a practical footgun or a grief vector in systems where approvals are broadly granted (e.g., management bots).
deployment_assumptions: NFT owner grants approval to an untrusted operator, or uses an automated system that can be coerced into calling with large arrays
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: withdrawBalances/withdrawBalancesTo are gated by ERC721 approval/ownership checks only; no explicit array-length cap in code (LiquidInfrastructureNFT.sol).

proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded loop over user-provided `erc20s`; external calls per entry (balanceOf, transfer)

preconditions: Caller is owner or approved for AccountId=1
Caller supplies an excessively large erc20s array causing out-of-gas

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: withdrawal can be made to consistently revert by using large arrays, blocking operational flows
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No max length checks; no pagination mechanism

analysis_questions: Is withdrawBalancesTo used by other contracts (e.g., the ERC20 aggregator) in a way that could be griefed by an approved operator changing thresholds to huge arrays?
What is the practical gas limit threshold (length) at which withdrawal reverts on target chain?
Can thresholdErc20s be set to an extremely large list and then used by the ERC20 aggregator to OOG in withdrawFromManagedNFTs?
validation_requirements: Gas test: call setThresholds with N entries and then withdraw via aggregator; find N where OOG occurs
Check whether any guards limit threshold sizes (none visible)
Model worst-case: for each entry, balanceOf+transfer external calls

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious approved operator sets thresholds to include thousands of ERC20 addresses. The ERC20 aggregator later calls getThresholds and passes that list to withdrawBalancesTo, causing out-of-gas and preventing revenue collection from that NFT, which can cascade into aggregator-level withdrawal disruption.
prerequisites: Operator approvals exist or are compromised
Ability to set large thresholds array on-chain

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate: revenue collection from affected NFT becomes unreliable; may degrade overall distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: ERC20 holders relying on aggregated withdrawals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium: can be fixed by resetting thresholds to a smaller list (if control recovered)

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for adversarially large thresholds/withdrawal lists and aggregator interaction

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Yes—determine maximum safe erc20 list length on target chain
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by repeated withdrawal reverts; not prevented by contract

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
